text.title: decision-tree-process Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for decision-tree-process. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Before listing any options, the decision MUST be stated as a single answerable question. The scope MUST include explicit out-of-scope items. The reversibility MUST be assessed: one-way door (high rigor required) or two-way door (lightweight process acceptable).
rule.rationale: Vague problem statements produce vague options and vague trade-off analyses. A single answerable question forces clarity and makes the decision auditable — either you answered it or you did not.

rule.statement: Options generation MUST include "do nothing" as an explicit option with documented costs. The list MUST include at least one unconventional approach. Elimination of options MUST be documented with reason. The shortlist MUST contain 2-5 options for analysis.
rule.rationale: Without "do nothing" as an option, teams systematically overestimate the value of change. Without unconventional options, teams miss breakthrough solutions. Without elimination rationale, the ADR is incomplete and future reviewers cannot reconstruct the reasoning.

rule.statement: The decision matrix MUST have criteria weights that sum to 100%. Weights MUST involve stakeholder input, not solo architect judgment. Each score MUST be evidence-based (benchmark, case study, direct experience) — not intuition. The matrix MUST identify at least one conflict between criteria (e.g., cost vs performance).
rule.rationale: Un-weighted criteria implicitly weight everything equally, which is almost never correct. Stakeholder-derived weights prevent the architect from making a values decision that belongs to the business. Evidence-based scores prevent rationalization after the fact.

rule.statement: High-impact decisions (significant cost, significant architectural change, or irreversible) MUST include a time-boxed POC with explicit success criteria before the decision is finalized. POC scope MUST be minimal: test only the critical integration points and performance-critical paths.
rule.rationale: Analysis alone cannot surface unknown unknowns. POCs reveal integration incompatibilities, performance surprises, and team capability gaps that no matrix can predict. Time-boxing prevents POC scope creep.

rule.statement: Every significant architecture decision MUST be documented as an ADR stored in version control (docs/adr/ or architecture/decisions/). The ADR MUST include: context, decision, alternatives considered, consequences (positive and negative), and the decision path (which options were eliminated and why).
rule.rationale: Without version-controlled ADRs, the reasoning behind decisions is lost when team members change. Teams relitigate the same decisions repeatedly, sometimes making the same mistakes. The ADR makes "why did we choose this?" answerable in seconds.
